wall.intro: The company operates under the same information constraints as a real UK energy supplier: it cannot see inside the simulation it runs in. It discovers its world only through observable interfaces -- market feeds, meter reads, bills, settlement statements -- and its models are approximations built from those observations, never reads of ground truth. Below are four real crossings of that wall: on each, the SIM's ground truth on one side, the COMPANY's own view on the other, and the honest gap between them. The gap is not a defect -- it is the wall working. A supplier that could see the truth would not be a supplier; it would be cheating.
wall.band_note: RAG below is divergence MAGNITUDE, not a verdict (R12: a metric is a diagnostic, never a target). It says how wide the gap is, so you can see it at a glance -- a wide gap that is correctly priced is the design, not a bug.

crossings.name: Forward-curve basis risk
crossings.subtitle: The hedge wall -- the company prices its own forward curve; it never reads the sim's.
crossings.basis: £/MWh forward price, point-in-time as-known (Point-in-Time Blindfold enforced -- the company cannot see future settlement)
crossings.mechanism: The company constructs its forward curve from observable market data only (a trailing mean plus a risk premium) and hedges against it. The sim holds the ground-truth forward. The company can never read it -- so it carries the gap as real basis risk, exactly as a licensed supplier does.
crossings.series_unit: mean forecast error, £/MWh (company premium vs realised outturn)

crossings.name: Demand estimation
crossings.subtitle: The volume wall -- the company forecasts annual volume at renewal; the sim knows the outturn.
crossings.divergence_label: Mean / max absolute forecast error (latest year)
crossings.basis: % error, forecast annual volume vs actual outturn (billed-volume basis)
crossings.mechanism: At renewal the company estimates each account's forward annual volume from its own prior billing -- it cannot query the sim's true forward demand. The mean absolute error is the volume-side wall gap it prices and hedges against.

crossings.name: Meter reads: estimated vs actual
crossings.subtitle: The billing wall -- the company bills partly on estimates; the sim holds true consumption.
crossings.basis: read-months, share billed on estimated vs actual reads (mean read delay 3.94 days)
crossings.mechanism: Where a real read is not yet in, the company bills on an ESTIMATE and cannot ask the sim for the truth. UK settlement then corrects it: the BSC run ladder (SF -> R1 -> R2 -> R3 -> RF) progressively swaps estimates for actuals over ~28 months, each run a credit/debit adjustment the company observes via its PCAN statements -- never by reading the sim's meter.

crossings.name: Point-in-time blindfold
crossings.subtitle: The time wall -- the sim holds the whole realised price path; the company sees only up to now.
crossings.company_view_unit: £/MWh mean, pre-crisis calm the company priced within
crossings.basis: £/MWh system sell price (SSP), real Elexon settlement; means are annual
crossings.mechanism: The sim is settled half-hourly against the REAL Elexon SSP series and holds the entire realised path, crisis and all. The company is blindfolded to the future: it prices and hedges on what was knowable at time T. It survives the 2021-22 spike (which killed ~30 real UK suppliers) because it priced under blindness, not because it saw it coming.

anchors.intro: The anchors register: the real external data the sim is calibrated against. Runtime calibration compares the sim's own outcomes to published Ofgem/DESNZ benchmarks and keeps the RAG rating as measured; the assumption library is the human-readable provenance trail, each row a sim value against an industry benchmark with its source and a checked status. Where a status is a warning or an open refresh, it is shown, not smoothed.

cards.note: SIM portfolio is predominantly I&C (B2B contract churn) vs Ofgem residential switching. Long-run average comparison is more robust than single-year with N<15 renewals.

sections.section: Customer Satisfaction Population Distribution (2026-07-10, discovery-agent tasked query re: simulation/sim_satisfaction.py)

rows.assumption: Population satisfaction distribution shape (GB domestic energy, 5-point Likert, all respondents)
rows.sim_value: sim_satisfaction.py: fixed BASELINE_SATISFACTION=0.70 for every customer; only 3 coarse step-adjustments possible (bill-shock -0.10 each, income-stress tier 0/-0.05/-0.15, tenure bonus +0.10 capped); live measurement across the current customer sample: 67% of datapoints exactly 0.70, 28% exactly 0.65, 5% other — no continuous per-customer variation exists anywhere in the model
rows.benchmark: Wave 20 (fieldwork Jan 2025, n=3,854, representative GB domestic bill-payers): Very satisfied 38%, Satisfied 42%, Neither satisfied nor dissatisfied 13%, Dissatisfied+Very dissatisfied 6%, Unsure/prefer not to say ~1%. Historical range across all 20 waves since tracking began (Oct/Nov 2018 to Jan 2025): net satisfied swings continuously from 66% (low point, Feb/Mar 2022, energy-crisis era) to 81% (all-time high, Jan 2025); net dissatisfied ranges 6%-21% over the same period. Critically, the "satisfied" outcome band alone splits into two materially different sub-levels (very satisfied 38% vs satisfied 42%, roughly a 47/53 split) even though these respondents share the same top-line "satisfied" classification — direct real-world evidence that people who would land in the SAME coarse bucket under a discrete model still spread across a continuum underneath it.
rows.source: Ofgem/Citizens Advice "Energy Consumer Satisfaction Survey" Wave 20, prepared by BMG Research for Ofgem and Citizens Advice, published May 2025, fieldwork conducted Jan 2025 (Energy Consumer Satisfaction Survey – Findings Report - January 2025_0.pdf, ofgem.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2025-05/, fetched 2026-07-10); supplier-level companion report Customers' satisfaction with their supplier - supplier level findings, publication date 2 Dec 2025, July-Aug 2025 wave (fetched 2026-07-10)

rows.assumption: Satisfaction gap by income-stress / financial-vulnerability proxy
rows.sim_value: sim_satisfaction.py income-stress step: exactly 3 discrete tiers (0 / -0.05 / -0.15), zero variance within a tier
rows.benchmark: "Doing well" financial cohort 89% satisfied vs "financially vulnerable" cohort 66% satisfied (Jan 2025) — a real ~23-point gap confirming the SIM's penalty direction — but both cohort-level percentages are themselves built by aggregating individuals spread across the full 5-point scale, not a shared per-person value; "financially vulnerable" and "highly financially vulnerable" are in fact two separate DESNZ/BMG-defined tiers in the underlying data (not modelled as 2 further sub-levels here)
rows.source: Same Wave 20 report, Executive Summary and "Though consumers continue to face some challenges" section

rows.assumption: Satisfaction gap by payment method (candidate future per-customer modifier)
rows.sim_value: Not currently wired into sim_satisfaction.py (a separate payment_channel_for_customer() archetype exists in household_segments.py but isn't consumed by the satisfaction model)
rows.benchmark: Direct Debit 82% satisfied, Prepayment meter 80%, Standard Credit 76% (Jan 2025) — a persistent ~6pt gap; Standard Credit has been the lowest-satisfaction payment cohort in every wave since tracking began, though the gap has narrowed over time (was 71% vs 66% DD/Standard-Credit in Aug/Sep 2023)
rows.source: Same Wave 20 report, "Satisfaction by payment type" section, Figure 3

rows.assumption: Inter-supplier satisfaction spread (secondary/complementary evidence — supplier-level, NOT a per-customer distribution)
rows.sim_value: n/a — no per-supplier concept in this single-supplier SIM
rows.benchmark: Citizens Advice Star Rating league table (live page, fetched 2026-07-10): 16 GB domestic energy suppliers ranked, overall star score range 1.38–4.33 out of 5 (100Green highest at 4.33, TruEnergy lowest at 1.38); mean 3.15/5 (0.63 normalized to a 0-1 scale), sample stdev 0.75/5 (0.149 normalized) across the 16 supplier scores
rows.source: Citizens Advice "Compare energy suppliers' customer service" league table (citizensadvice.org.uk/consumer/energy/energy-supply/get-a-better-energy-deal/compare-domestic-energy-suppliers-customer-service/, live HTML table fetched 2026-07-10, ranks and scores read directly from the page's supplier-table rows); methodology note: "Decision document on revised methodology for the Citizens Advice star rating", Aug 2025 (citizensadvice.org.uk)

rows.assumption: Kraken cost-to-serve reduction from unified monolith
rows.benchmark: "30-40% operational cost reduction claimed"
rows.source: COMPETITOR_PLATFORMS_2026.md §4 (director-supplied, AI-assisted, unsourced)

rows.benchmark: "17+ autonomous AI agents in live production"

sections.section: Smart Meter Half-Hourly Data Access: Billing vs. Settlement Consent (2026-07-10, director challenge to tools/generate_customer_consumption.py docstring)

rows.assumption: Supplier access to smart meter reads for its OWN billing purposes
rows.sim_value: tools/generate_customer_consumption.py docstring treats "HH data sharing to supplier" as one undifferentiated opt-in most customers decline
rows.benchmark: NOT opt-in gated — a DCC-enrolled smart meter in smart mode (~90% of installed smart meters, DESNZ Q4 2024, already anchored above as SMART_METER_NOT_COMMUNICATING_RATE=0.10) routinely sends reads to its own supplier for billing by default; the only real "opt-out" is losing/declining smart functionality (traditional mode), which few do
rows.source: DESNZ Q4 2024 Smart Meters Statistics (existing anchor, this doc)

rows.assumption: Supplier access to HH-granularity data specifically for market-wide SETTLEMENT purposes
rows.sim_value: Not modelled as a distinct mechanism
rows.benchmark: A genuine, narrower opt-in (domestic) / opt-out (microbusiness) consent regime DOES exist in UK regulation for this specific purpose, unchanged since 2018; no published uptake percentage found (Ofgem's 2018 survey measured stated willingness ~66%, not actual consent given)
rows.source: Ofgem "Decision for access to half-hourly electricity data for settlement purposes" (25 Jun 2019); Ofgem "Consumer views on sharing half-hourly settlement data" (10 Jul 2018)

rows.benchmark: Ofgem/Elexon programme replacing profile-class settlement with universal HH settlement for ALL customers by infrastructure migration, not per-customer consent — migration phase began Sept 2025 (18-month window), full go-live due by May 2027; this retires the settlement opt-in mechanism above
rows.source: Energy UK "Energy UK Explains: Market-wide Half Hourly Settlement" (9 Oct 2024, updated 24 Oct 2024)

sections.section: B1 Margin Bridge — Real UK Energy Supplier Practice (2026-07-10, Maturity Map DISCOVER, re: saas/reporting/margin_attribution.py)

rows.assumption: Gross margin bridge driver granularity — generic FP&A price/volume/mix convention
rows.sim_value: margin_attribution.py treats gross margin YoY delta as ONE lump driver, not split into price/volume/mix
rows.benchmark: Price/volume/mix decomposition (technically "Sales Mix Variance" + "Sales Quantity Variance" = "Sales Volume Variance", alongside a separate "Flexible Budget Variance" capturing price/rate effects) IS a standard, long-established FP&A/management-accounting convention — but this is a GENERIC cross-industry technique (illustrated with an airline-ticket-pricing example in the source), not shown to be energy-retail-specific practice (see next row)
rows.source: CFI (Corporate Finance Institute) "Variance Analysis" and "Revenue Variance Analysis" (corporatefinanceinstitute.com/resources/accounting/variance-analysis/ and .../revenue-variance-analysis/, fetched 2026-07-10)

rows.assumption: Gross margin bridge driver granularity — REAL UK energy retail practice specifically
rows.sim_value: Same as above (one lump gross-margin driver)
rows.benchmark: Centrica plc's actual FY2025 preliminary results do NOT decompose Retail/Home Energy Supply EBITDA movement into price/volume/mix. Instead the real disclosure uses energy-sector-specific named drivers, separately quantified in £m where material: weather (£80m headwind, explicitly quantified), "shape of the commodity curve" (hedging/forward-position effect, described qualitatively), regulatory reconciliations (£42m final Energy Price Guarantee scheme reconciliation, £41m benefit from lower Feed-in-Tariff costs than previously recognised), general cost phasing, and a tariff-mix note kept QUALITATIVE only ("customers moving to fixed price products, typically at a discount to the standard variable tariff, reduced profitability compared to 2024")
rows.source: Centrica plc "2025 Preliminary Results Announcement" (centrica.com/media/2hjf1fyl/centrica-plc-2025-preliminary-results-announcement.pdf), pp.12-13, "Preliminary results \
rows.last_checked: Group overview" and "Financial Performance" sections, fetched 2026-07-10

rows.assumption: Bad debt movement as its own separately-quantified £ driver (cross-check against existing margin_attribution.py driver)
rows.sim_value: margin_attribution.py already carries bad debt delta as its own named driver
rows.benchmark: CONFIRMED as real management-reporting practice: Centrica's own CFO, live in the FY2025 results Q&A, quantifies the YoY bad debt movement explicitly as a standalone driver — "Our bad debt charge as a percentage of revenue went from 2.3 to 2.8% in the year. It's about 40 million pound increase charge."
rows.source: Centrica plc "2025 Preliminary Results Presentation and Q&A Transcript" (centrica.com/media/ba0by51l/centrica-2025-preliminary-results-presentation-and-qa-transcript.pdf), fetched 2026-07-10

rows.assumption: Customer-portfolio growth/churn as its own separately-quantified £ margin driver
rows.sim_value: margin_attribution.py tracks portfolio (active customer count) change as an INFORMATIONAL figure only — explicitly NOT an attributed £ driver
rows.benchmark: Centrica FY2025 discloses customer NUMBERS as a standalone KPI (UK Home Energy Supply grew from 7.46m to 7.50m underlying accounts; +91,000 customers gained via the Supplier-of-Last-Resort process following two competitor supplier failures, offsetting "a small decrease in underlying customers") presented ALONGSIDE, not INSIDE, the £ profit bridge. No isolated "£ contribution from new customers" vs "£ lost from churned customers" bridge line was found published anywhere in this document — the customer/tariff-mix effect on profit is narrated in PROSE only, never isolated to its own £m figure
rows.source: Same Centrica PR PDF, p.12, "Home Energy Supply customer numbers grew 1%..." paragraph and the following "Financial Performance" paragraph, fetched 2026-07-10

rows.assumption: Standard/common name for this report type
rows.sim_value: n/a — internal module named margin_attribution.py, no equivalent chart/report currently rendered on a business surface
rows.benchmark: "Waterfall chart" is the standard generic name for this exact chart type in financial reporting/analysis: "a dynamic, intuitive visualization that shows how a series of positive or negative changes impact an initial value, ultimately resulting in a final total... Identify drivers behind the final bar, such as total expenses or profit" (CFI). Confirmed in REAL energy-sector use: Centrica's own CFO/IR team use "waterfall chart" verbatim, live, in the FY2025 results Q&A, referring to their previously-published EBITDA-to-capex-to-net-debt bridge — "you previously published a waterfall chart... you came up with your EBITDA's, your capex's, your requirements" and later "the waterfall chart from last year in the financial framework." "EBITDA bridge"/"margin bridge"/"margin walk" are common informal synonyms for the same chart type in general market usage, but neither exact phrase was found verbatim in the specific Centrica documents fetched this session (their FY25 deck's £m walk is presented as narrative bullets in the press release, not a labelled chart, in the text layers this session could extract) — "waterfall chart" is the one term directly evidenced in a real energy supplier's own mouth
rows.source: CFI "How to Create a Waterfall Chart in Excel" (corporatefinanceinstitute.com/resources/financial-modeling/waterfall-chart-template/, fetched 2026-07-10); Centrica plc "2025 Preliminary Results Presentation and Q&A Transcript" (as above), exchange with Dominic Nash (Barclays) re: the "financial framework", fetched 2026-07-10

rows.assumption: CMA/Ofgem regulator-side profitability reporting — does it use a bridge/waterfall/price-volume-mix convention? (negative control)
rows.sim_value: n/a — checked as a negative control against the company-reporting findings above
rows.benchmark: NOT FOUND — CMA Energy Market Investigation (2016) Appendix 10.1 "Approach to profitability and financial analysis" and Appendix 10.2 "Retail profit margin analysis" (the UK competition regulator's own methodology for exactly this kind of YoY energy-supplier margin analysis) contain ZERO occurrences of "bridge", "waterfall", "price effect"/"volume effect"/"mix effect", or "decompos-" anywhere in their full extracted text — the regulator's approach is a levels-and-trends narrative (margin/ROCE/EBIT per year, cross-supplier comparison table), not a bridge/waterfall chart convention. Ofgem's 2025 "Financial Resilience Transparency Report" likewise contains no such terminology — it reports ring-fencing/credit-rating/liquidity metrics, not a margin bridge
rows.source: CMA "Energy market investigation: Final report", Appendices 10.1 and 10.2 (assets.digital.cabinet-office.gov.uk, 2016, fetched and full-text-searched 2026-07-10); Ofgem "FRC_transparency_report.pdf" (ofgem.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2025-04/, fetched and full-text-searched 2026-07-10)

sections.section: W1 Reveal-Over-Time — Point-in-Time Data Access Patterns in Quant Finance (2026-07-10, Maturity Map DISCOVER, code-review finding re: sim/risk_engine.py::calculate_sigma_recent vs company/trading/hedge_decision.py::estimate_price_volatility)

rows.benchmark: Source
rows.source: Confidence

rows.assumption: Q1: Is "look-ahead bias" the standard named term for a model/backtest seeing data from after its simulated "as of" date?
rows.sim_value: Confirmed, verbatim: "look ahead bias — The practice of making decisions using information that would not be available until some time in the future. Look-ahead bias invalidates backtest results because the algorithm uses data it would not have access to in live trading." This is the SAME failure class as the SIM's "Point-in-Time Blindfold" epistemic law, just named differently by industry.
rows.benchmark: QuantConnect/LEAN documentation, "Glossary" (quantconnect.com/docs/v2/writing-algorithms/key-concepts/glossary, anchor #18-look-ahead-bias), fetched 2026-07-10
rows.source: H — primary vendor documentation, exact quote fetched live

rows.assumption: Q1: How do real backtesting ENGINES architecturally prevent look-ahead bias (not just define it)?
rows.sim_value: "Backtesting platforms come in two general varieties, batch processing and event streaming. Batch processing... loads all data into an array and passes it to your algorithm for analysis. Because your algorithm has access to future data points, it is easy to introduce look-ahead bias... QuantConnect/LEAN is a streaming analysis system... you can't access price data beyond the Time Frontier." Zipline (the other major open-source Python backtester, ex-Quantopian): "Stream-based: Process each event individually, avoids look-ahead bias" is listed as a core architectural benefit, not an optional safeguard.
rows.benchmark: QuantConnect docs, "Key Concepts → Algorithm Engine" (quantconnect.com/docs/v2/writing-algorithms/key-concepts/algorithm-engine), fetched 2026-07-10; Zipline docs, "Beginner Tutorial" (zipline.ml4trading.io/beginner-tutorial.html), fetched 2026-07-10
rows.source: H — both primary, current vendor/project documentation, exact quotes fetched live

rows.assumption: Q2: Is "self-contained function takes an explicit as-of date and filters internally" (SIM's calculate_sigma_recent pattern) the recommended approach vs. "trust the caller to pre-slice"?
rows.sim_value: Directly evidenced as the recommended direction, though the more mature real-world pattern goes one step further: a dedicated, uniformly-enforced ACCESS LAYER that no caller can bypass. The named real pattern for this is a "point-in-time join" (aka "point-in-time correct" retrieval): "Feast is able to join features from one or more feature views onto an entity dataframe in a point-in-time correct way. This means Feast is able to reproduce the state of features at a specific point in the past... Feast will scan backward in time from the entity dataframe timestamp up to a maximum of the TTL time specified." The whole point of the abstraction is that an individual feature/table author never has to remember to truncate — the join engine enforces it for every caller, every time.
rows.benchmark: Feast (Linux-Foundation-hosted open-source ML feature store), "Concepts → Point-in-time joins" (docs.feast.dev/getting-started/concepts/point-in-time-joins), fetched 2026-07-10
rows.source: H — primary, current, open-source project documentation, exact quote fetched live

rows.assumption: Q2 (continued): is there a broader, non-ML-specific software-architecture name for "as-of" data access with retroactive-correction handling?
rows.sim_value: Yes — "bitemporal history": "Bitemporal history treats time as two dimensions: actual history records what history should be given perfect transmission of information, while record history captures how our knowledge of history changes." This is the standard enterprise-data-architecture term (predates ML feature stores) for a data model that always supports "what did we know, as of date X" queries safely.
rows.benchmark: Martin Fowler (ThoughtWorks Chief Scientist, widely-cited enterprise-architecture-patterns author), "Bitemporal History" (martinfowler.com/articles/bitemporal-history.html), published 7 April 2021, fetched 2026-07-10
rows.source: H — primary, well-known, individually-attributed software-architecture source, exact quote fetched live

rows.assumption: Q2 (continued): is there a standard finance-ML TEXTBOOK treatment, specifically Lopez de Prado, of preventing exactly this leakage?
rows.sim_value: Confirmed via the publisher's own table of contents: Chapter 7, "Cross-Validation in Finance" — "7.3 Why K-Fold CV Fails in Finance", "7.4 A Solution: Purged K-Fold CV", "7.4.1 Purging the Training Set", "7.4.2 Embargo", "7.4.3 The Purged K-Fold Class". "Purging" removes training observations whose label window overlaps the test window; "embargo" adds a further buffer after the test window before training resumes. Both are implemented as a NAMED, reusable class (PurgedKFoldCV) in the accompanying mlfinlab code, not as an inline caller-side patch.
rows.benchmark: Marcos López de Prado, "Advances in Financial Machine Learning" (Wiley, 2018, ISBN 978-1-119-48208-6) — publisher table of contents (wiley.com/en-us/Advances+in+Financial+Machine+Learning-p-9781119482086), fetched 2026-07-10
rows.source: H — primary publisher record (table of contents fetched live); book's full internal text not independently fetched this session, so the chapter's argumentative detail is stated from established general knowledge of the book, not a page-level quote

rows.assumption: Q1 (context): is "walk-forward" backtesting a real, standard, independently named methodology?
rows.sim_value: Confirmed: "Walk Forward Analysis was presented by Robert E. Pardo in his book 'Design, Testing and Optimization of Trading Systems' in 1992... now widely considered the 'gold standard' in trading strategy validation," with in-sample/out-of-sample windows that never overlap and always roll strictly forward in time.
rows.benchmark: Wikipedia, "Walk forward optimization" (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Walk_forward_optimization), citing Robert E. Pardo's 1992/2008 book, fetched 2026-07-10
rows.source: M — tertiary source (Wikipedia) naming a real, independently-verifiable primary book; book itself not directly fetched this session

rows.assumption: Q3: real vendor evidence that "point-in-time" as a PRODUCT CATEGORY exists specifically because ad hoc/inconsistent data access caused real backtesting errors
rows.sim_value: Confirmed the product family is real and named exactly this: "Compustat Data including Compustat North America, Compustat International, Compustat Global, and Compustat Point-in-Time data sets." The existence of a distinctly-branded "Point-in-Time" product line (separate from the ordinary Compustat feed) is itself evidence that ordinary (non-PIT, restated) financial databases were found to distort backtests badly enough that the industry built and sells a structurally different product to fix it.
rows.benchmark: Wikipedia, "Compustat" (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Compustat), fetched 2026-07-10 — S&P Global's own Compustat Point-in-Time product pages returned HTTP 403 (bot-blocked) when fetched directly this session
rows.source: M — tertiary source confirms the real, named product exists; could not fetch S&P Global's own primary product description this session (genuine access gap, noted not invented)

rows.assumption: Q3: any DIRECTLY quantified real-world financial damage from using non-point-in-time data in backtests?
rows.sim_value: Yes, for the closely related "survivorship bias" failure mode (a different but sibling class of point-in-time data error — using a database that has QUIETLY DROPPED failed entities, rather than seeing FUTURE data): "Elton, Gruber, and Blake showed that survivorship bias is larger in the small-fund sector than in large mutual funds... They estimate the size of the bias across the U.S. mutual fund industry as 0.9% per annum" of overstated risk-adjusted return. This is a real, quantified, published academic finding of material financial distortion from a non-point-in-time data assembly practice.
rows.benchmark: Wikipedia, "Survivorship bias" §"Finance and economics" (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Survivorship_bias), citing Elton, Gruber & Blake (1996), fetched 2026-07-10; underlying journal article not independently re-fetched this session
rows.source: M — tertiary source citing a real, well-known, independently-verifiable academic study; the 1996 paper itself not directly re-fetched

rows.assumption: Q3: known incident SPECIFICALLY matching "some code paths/functions in one codebase enforce a safety invariant, others silently trust the caller, and this inconsistency causes real damage"?
rows.sim_value: Explicit negative finding — searched this session, no case study was located that names look-ahead bias specifically as the cause and frames it as "inconsistent per-function enforcement within one codebase." The closest REAL, extremely well-documented analogue found (a different bug class — dead/stale code inconsistently present across servers, not look-ahead bias) is Knight Capital's 1 August 2012 trading-system failure: a deployment error left OLD "Power Peg" code live on ONE of eight production servers (the new code had been correctly deployed to the other seven) while a repurposed order-routing flag inadvertently re-triggered that one server's dead code path, causing 4 million erroneous executions across 154 stocks in ~45 minutes and a $440m pre-tax loss. The structural lesson — an inconsistency between what different parts of a codebase "know" is safe, left undetected until a live trigger — is analogous in KIND (not degree or domain) to the risk named in the code-review finding, but this incident is honestly NOT a look-ahead-bias case and should not be cited as if it were.
rows.benchmark: Wikipedia, "Knight Capital Group" §"2012 stock trading disruption" (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Knight_Capital_Group), fetched 2026-07-10 — facts independently corroborated by extensive contemporaneous press and the SEC's own 2013 administrative order (SEC Release No. 70694, not independently re-fetched this session)
rows.source: H for the facts of the Knight Capital incident itself (extremely well-documented, multiply-sourced public event); explicitly NOT a look-ahead-bias precedent — flagged L/not-applicable for that specific claim

sections.section: Settlement Timetable & Rebilling Cascade / DD Mid-Cycle Correction (2026-07-12, discovery-agent tasked query re: M2 settlement timetable / DD cash-engine build)

rows.assumption: Wholesale settlement is finalised once, at/near the trading day
rows.sim_value: W3_2_settlement_timetable not yet built (docs/design/maturity_map.yaml, level_current=0, evidence=[])
rows.benchmark: [M] Each Settlement Date is re-run MULTIPLE times over an extended tail (historically II→SF→R1→R2→R3→(DF)→RF, spanning days to ~12-14 months under the pre-2022 Standard Settlement Timetable; November 2022 Reconciliation Reform shortened this for most meter types) — later runs use progressively better metering data and can revise the volume/imbalance cost allocated to a supplier for an already-past trading day
rows.source: Elexon BSC Section T / Settlement Calendar — training-era recall, NOT fetched live this session (site Cloudflare-walled)

rows.assumption: Settlement-run revisions directly trigger customer-level rebills
rows.sim_value: Not modelled as a distinct mechanism; risk of conflating with the already-built D3 catch-up-rebilling atom (estimated→actual meter read)
rows.benchmark: [M] They do NOT — wholesale settlement revisions are a PORTFOLIO-level cost/revenue true-up absorbed into the supplier's own books and future tariff-setting; customer-level catch-up rebilling (estimated vs actual meter read) is a separate retail mechanism bounded by the Ofgem back-billing cap, unrelated to which Elexon run number produced the wholesale figures
rows.source: General BSC/SVA structure — training-era recall

rows.assumption: Direct Debit collection amount can be corrected mid-cycle once already submitted to Bacs
rows.sim_value: Not yet modelled; simulation/bacs_rails.py/dd_collection_book.py (W5_1_banking_payment_rails, level 2) already model the ~3-working-day submission/processing/collection cycle accurately
rows.benchmark: [M] No — Bacs Direct Debit Guarantee rules require advance notice of any change to amount/date/frequency before an in-flight collection can be altered; [L] commonly-cited default notice period is 10 working days "or as otherwise agreed" (many billers negotiate shorter). A correction discovered mid-pipeline must land on the NEXT collection cycle, not the one already submitted
rows.source: Bacs/Pay.UK Direct Debit Guarantee terms — training-era recall, not fetched live

rows.sim_value: docs/design/maturity_map.yaml D3 atom cites BOTH "Ofgem SLC 31A" (2026-07-11 entry) and "SLC 21BA" (2026-07-12 entry) for the same 12-month cap mechanism
rows.benchmark: [L] Best recollection: the substantive domestic back-billing condition is generally SLC 21B/21BA ("Back-Billing"), not 31A — could not confirm independently this session (Ofgem's own back-billing guidance URL 404'd)
rows.source: Ofgem consolidated Standard Licence Conditions (domestic supply) — not located/verified this session
